title: Practical Patterns & Recipes
description: 30 copy-pasteable Android recipes: power, networking, data, UI, lifecycle, Compose, architecture, and testing

references.title: Android Developers — Guide to App Architecture

lesson:
Power & Battery Recipes

Recipe 1: Check Battery State and Adapt Behavior

Use when you need to defer heavy work unless the device is charging or has sufficient battery.

// No BroadcastReceiver needed — read the sticky intent directly fun getBatteryInfo(context: Context): BatteryInfo { val batteryStatus: Intent? = IntentFilter(Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED).let { filter -> context.registerReceiver(null, filter) // null receiver = read sticky } val level = batteryStatus?.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_LEVEL, -1) ?: -1 val scale = batteryStatus?.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_SCALE, -1) ?: -1 val batteryPct = if (level >= 0 && scale > 0) level * 100 / scale else -1 val status = batteryStatus?.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_STATUS, -1) ?: -1 val isCharging = status == BatteryManager.BATTERY_STATUS_CHARGING || status == BatteryManager.BATTERY_STATUS_FULL val chargePlug = batteryStatus?.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_PLUGGED, -1) ?: -1 val isUsb = chargePlug == BatteryManager.BATTERY_PLUGGED_USB val isAc = chargePlug == BatteryManager.BATTERY_PLUGGED_AC return BatteryInfo(batteryPct, isCharging, isUsb, isAc) } data class BatteryInfo( val percent: Int, val isCharging: Boolean, val isUsb: Boolean, val isAc: Boolean ) // Usage: skip heavy sync if battery is low and not charging fun maybeSyncData(context: Context) { val battery = getBatteryInfo(context) if (battery.isCharging || battery.percent > 20) { startSync() } else { Log.d("Sync", "Skipping — battery at ${battery.percent}%, not charging") } }

The sticky intent for ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED is always available. Passing null as the receiver returns the last broadcast without registering a listener.

Recipe 2: Register for Power State Changes

Use when you want to start/stop background work based on charging state.

class PowerStateReceiver : BroadcastReceiver() { override fun onReceive(context: Context, intent: Intent) { when (intent.action) { Intent.ACTION_POWER_CONNECTED -> { Log.d("Power", "Charging started — enable background sync") WorkManager.getInstance(context) .enqueueUniquePeriodicWork( "bg-sync", ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.KEEP, buildSyncRequest() ) } Intent.ACTION_POWER_DISCONNECTED -> { Log.d("Power", "Unplugged — cancel background sync") WorkManager.getInstance(context) .cancelUniqueWork("bg-sync") } } } } // Register in AndroidManifest.xml: // <receiver android:name=".PowerStateReceiver" android:exported="false"> // <intent-filter> // <action android:name="android.intent.action.ACTION_POWER_CONNECTED" /> // <action android:name="android.intent.action.ACTION_POWER_DISCONNECTED" /> // </intent-filter> // </receiver> // Or register dynamically in an Activity/Service: val filter = IntentFilter().apply { addAction(Intent.ACTION_POWER_CONNECTED) addAction(Intent.ACTION_POWER_DISCONNECTED) } registerReceiver(PowerStateReceiver(), filter)

Manifest-registered receivers survive process death. Dynamic registration is scoped to the component's lifecycle.

Recipe 3: WorkManager with Battery-Optimal Constraints

Use when you want work to run only under ideal conditions (Wi-Fi + charging + idle).

fun buildOptimalSyncRequest(): PeriodicWorkRequest { val constraints = Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.UNMETERED) // Wi-Fi only .setRequiresCharging(true) // plugged in .setRequiresDeviceIdle(true) // user not active .setRequiresBatteryNotLow(true) // battery > 15% .build() return PeriodicWorkRequestBuilder<SyncWorker>( repeatInterval = 6, TimeUnit.HOURS, flexInterval = 30, TimeUnit.MINUTES // run within last 30min of window ) .setConstraints(constraints) .setBackoffCriteria( BackoffPolicy.EXPONENTIAL, WorkRequest.MIN_BACKOFF_MILLIS, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS ) .addTag("optimal-sync") .build() } class SyncWorker( appContext: Context, params: WorkerParameters ) : CoroutineWorker(appContext, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { return try { val repo = (applicationContext as MyApp).repository repo.syncPendingChanges() Result.success() } catch (e: Exception) { if (runAttemptCount < 3) Result.retry() else Result.failure() } } } // Enqueue once at app startup: WorkManager.getInstance(context).enqueueUniquePeriodicWork( "optimal-sync", ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.KEEP, // don't replace if already scheduled buildOptimalSyncRequest() )

The flex interval means the work runs in the last 30 minutes of each 6-hour window. Constraints are AND-ed -- all must be met before WorkManager starts the worker.

Recipe 4: Batch Network Writes with Periodic Sync

Use to collect mutations locally and flush them to the server in a single batch, reducing wake-ups and network calls.

// 1. Room entity for pending writes @Entity(tableName = "pending_writes") data class PendingWrite( @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Long = 0, val endpoint: String, val payload: String, // JSON body val createdAt: Long = System.currentTimeMillis() ) @Dao interface PendingWriteDao { @Insert suspend fun insert(write: PendingWrite) @Query("SELECT * FROM pending_writes ORDER BY createdAt ASC LIMIT 50") suspend fun getOldestBatch(): List<PendingWrite> @Delete suspend fun delete(writes: List<PendingWrite>) @Query("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM pending_writes") suspend fun count(): Int } // 2. Repository: save locally, never hit network directly class WriteRepository( private val dao: PendingWriteDao, private val workManager: WorkManager ) { suspend fun enqueueWrite(endpoint: String, payload: String) { dao.insert(PendingWrite(endpoint = endpoint, payload = payload)) // Schedule flush if we hit batch threshold if (dao.count() >= 10) { workManager.enqueueUniqueWork( "flush-writes", ExistingWorkPolicy.KEEP, OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<FlushWorker>().build() ) } } } // 3. Worker: flush batch to server class FlushWorker( ctx: Context, params: WorkerParameters ) : CoroutineWorker(ctx, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { val dao = (applicationContext as MyApp).database.pendingWriteDao() val api = (applicationContext as MyApp).api while (true) { val batch = dao.getOldestBatch() if (batch.isEmpty()) break try { api.submitBatch(batch.map { BatchItem(it.endpoint, it.payload) }) dao.delete(batch) } catch (e: Exception) { return if (runAttemptCount < 3) Result.retry() else Result.failure() } } return Result.success() } }

This pattern trades real-time writes for battery efficiency. Writes are instant (local Room insert), network flushes are batched. The worker drains the queue in pages of 50.

Recipe 5: Location Batching

Use to reduce wake-ups by letting the system batch location updates and deliver them together.

class LocationRepository( private val fusedClient: FusedLocationProviderClient ) { @SuppressLint("MissingPermission") fun batchedLocationUpdates(): Flow<Location> = callbackFlow { val request = LocationRequest.Builder( Priority.PRIORITY_BALANCED_POWER_ACCURACY, 60_000L // desired interval: 60s ).apply { setMinUpdateDistanceMeters(50f) // skip if < 50m moved setMaxUpdateDelayMillis(300_000L) // batch up to 5 minutes setMinUpdateIntervalMillis(30_000L) // never faster than 30s }.build() val callback = object : LocationCallback() { override fun onLocationResult(result: LocationResult) { for (location in result.locations) { trySend(location) } } } fusedClient.requestLocationUpdates( request, callback, Looper.getMainLooper() ) awaitClose { fusedClient.removeLocationUpdates(callback) } } } // Collect in a lifecycle-aware way: lifecycleScope.launch { repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) { locationRepo.batchedLocationUpdates().collect { location -> Log.d("Loc", "${location.latitude}, ${location.longitude}") } } }

setMaxUpdateDelayMillis is the key: it tells the system to batch updates and deliver them all at once, keeping the CPU asleep between batches.

Networking Recipes

Recipe 6: Retrofit + OkHttp Setup with Auth Interceptor and Retry

Use as the standard networking setup for any Android app.

// Auth interceptor — adds Bearer token to every request class AuthInterceptor( private val tokenProvider: () -> String? ) : Interceptor { override fun intercept(chain: Interceptor.Chain): Response { val original = chain.request() val token = tokenProvider() val request = if (token != null) { original.newBuilder() .header("Authorization", "Bearer $token") .build() } else original return chain.proceed(request) } } // Retry interceptor — retries on 5xx or IOException class RetryInterceptor(private val maxRetries: Int = 3) : Interceptor { override fun intercept(chain: Interceptor.Chain): Response { var lastException: IOException? = null repeat(maxRetries) { attempt -> try { val response = chain.proceed(chain.request()) if (response.isSuccessful || response.code !in 500..599) { return response } response.close() } catch (e: IOException) { lastException = e } Thread.sleep((1000L * (1 shl attempt)).coerceAtMost(8000L)) } throw lastException ?: IOException("Retry failed after $maxRetries attempts") } } // OkHttpClient val okHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder() .addInterceptor(AuthInterceptor { tokenStore.getAccessToken() }) .addInterceptor(RetryInterceptor(maxRetries = 3)) .addInterceptor(HttpLoggingInterceptor().apply { level = HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.BODY }) .connectTimeout(15, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .readTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .build() // Retrofit val retrofit = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/v1/") .client(okHttpClient) .addConverterFactory(Json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType())) .build() // API interface interface ApiService { @GET("users/{id}") suspend fun getUser(@Path("id") id: String): UserDto @POST("users") suspend fun createUser(@Body user: CreateUserRequest): UserDto @GET("posts") suspend fun getPosts( @Query("page") page: Int, @Query("limit") limit: Int = 20 ): List<PostDto> } val api: ApiService = retrofit.create(ApiService::class.java)

Interceptors are chained: Auth runs first (adds token), then Retry (handles failures), then Logging (shows final request). Application interceptors see the original request; network interceptors see the request after redirects.

Recipe 7: Exponential Backoff Retry (Suspend Function)

Use for retrying any suspend function with exponential delay.

suspend fun <T> retryWithBackoff( maxRetries: Int = 3, initialDelayMs: Long = 1000L, maxDelayMs: Long = 16_000L, factor: Double = 2.0, block: suspend () -> T ): T { var currentDelay = initialDelayMs repeat(maxRetries - 1) { attempt -> try { return block() } catch (e: Exception) { Log.w("Retry", "Attempt ${attempt + 1} failed: ${e.message}") } delay(currentDelay) currentDelay = (currentDelay * factor).toLong().coerceAtMost(maxDelayMs) } return block() // last attempt — let it throw } // Usage: val user = retryWithBackoff(maxRetries = 4, initialDelayMs = 500L) { api.getUser(userId) }

The last attempt runs without try-catch so the exception propagates to the caller. delay() is cancellation-aware, so cancelling the coroutine stops the retry loop immediately.

Recipe 8: Sealed ApiResult with safeApiCall Wrapper

Use to wrap every API call with a consistent success/error type.

sealed interface ApiResult<out T> { data class Success<T>(val data: T) : ApiResult<T> data class Error(val code: Int, val message: String) : ApiResult<Nothing> data class Exception(val throwable: Throwable) : ApiResult<Nothing> } suspend fun <T> safeApiCall(block: suspend () -> T): ApiResult<T> { return try { ApiResult.Success(block()) } catch (e: retrofit2.HttpException) { ApiResult.Error( code = e.code(), message = e.response()?.errorBody()?.string() ?: e.message() ) } catch (e: java.io.IOException) { ApiResult.Exception(e) // network error, timeout } catch (e: kotlin.Exception) { ApiResult.Exception(e) // serialization, unexpected } } // Usage in ViewModel: class UserViewModel(private val api: ApiService) : ViewModel() { private val _state = MutableStateFlow<UiState<User>>(UiState.Loading) val state: StateFlow<UiState<User>> = _state.asStateFlow() fun loadUser(id: String) { viewModelScope.launch { _state.value = UiState.Loading _state.value = when (val result = safeApiCall { api.getUser(id) }) { is ApiResult.Success -> UiState.Success(result.data) is ApiResult.Error -> UiState.Error("Server error ${result.code}") is ApiResult.Exception -> UiState.Error("Network error") } } } } sealed interface UiState<out T> { data object Loading : UiState<Nothing> data class Success<T>(val data: T) : UiState<T> data class Error(val message: String) : UiState<Nothing> }

The sealed interface means the when is exhaustive -- the compiler forces you to handle every case. Separating HttpException from IOException lets you show "server error" vs "check your connection" messages.

Recipe 9: Paging 3 with RemoteMediator (Database-Backed Pagination)

Use for feeds that cache pages in Room and fetch from the network as the user scrolls.

// Entity @Entity(tableName = "articles") data class ArticleEntity( @PrimaryKey val id: String, val title: String, val body: String, val createdAt: Long ) @Dao interface ArticleDao { @Query("SELECT * FROM articles ORDER BY createdAt DESC") fun pagingSource(): PagingSource<Int, ArticleEntity> @Upsert suspend fun upsertAll(articles: List<ArticleEntity>) @Query("DELETE FROM articles") suspend fun clearAll() } // RemoteMediator — bridges network and database @OptIn(ExperimentalPagingApi::class) class ArticleRemoteMediator( private val api: ApiService, private val db: AppDatabase ) : RemoteMediator<Int, ArticleEntity>() { override suspend fun load( loadType: LoadType, state: PagingState<Int, ArticleEntity> ): MediatorResult { val page = when (loadType) { LoadType.REFRESH -> 1 LoadType.PREPEND -> return MediatorResult.Success(endOfPaginationReached = true) LoadType.APPEND -> { val lastItem = state.lastItemOrNull() ?: return MediatorResult.Success(endOfPaginationReached = true) // Calculate next page from last item (state.pages.size + 1) } } return try { val response = api.getArticles(page = page, limit = state.config.pageSize) db.withTransaction { if (loadType == LoadType.REFRESH) { db.articleDao().clearAll() } db.articleDao().upsertAll(response.map { it.toEntity() }) } MediatorResult.Success(endOfPaginationReached = response.isEmpty()) } catch (e: Exception) { MediatorResult.Error(e) } } } // ViewModel — wires it all together @OptIn(ExperimentalPagingApi::class) class ArticleViewModel( private val api: ApiService, private val db: AppDatabase ) : ViewModel() { val articles: Flow<PagingData<ArticleEntity>> = Pager( config = PagingConfig(pageSize = 20, prefetchDistance = 5), remoteMediator = ArticleRemoteMediator(api, db), pagingSourceFactory = { db.articleDao().pagingSource() } ).flow.cachedIn(viewModelScope) }

RemoteMediator loads from the network and writes to Room. The PagingSource reads from Room. Room automatically invalidates the PagingSource when data changes, so the UI updates. This gives you offline support for free.

Data & Offline Recipes

Recipe 10: Room Entity + DAO + Database with Flow

Use as the foundation for any local persistence.

@Entity( tableName = "tasks", indices = [Index(value = ["status"])] ) data class TaskEntity( @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Long = 0, val title: String, val description: String = "", val status: String = "pending", // pending, done val createdAt: Long = System.currentTimeMillis(), val updatedAt: Long = System.currentTimeMillis() ) @Dao interface TaskDao { @Query("SELECT * FROM tasks ORDER BY createdAt DESC") fun observeAll(): Flow<List<TaskEntity>> // auto-emits on changes @Query("SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE id = :id") fun observeById(id: Long): Flow<TaskEntity?> @Query("SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE status = :status") fun observeByStatus(status: String): Flow<List<TaskEntity>> @Insert(onConflict = OnConflictStrategy.REPLACE) suspend fun upsert(task: TaskEntity): Long @Delete suspend fun delete(task: TaskEntity) @Query("UPDATE tasks SET status = :status, updatedAt = :now WHERE id = :id") suspend fun updateStatus(id: Long, status: String, now: Long = System.currentTimeMillis()) @Query("DELETE FROM tasks WHERE status = 'done'") suspend fun clearCompleted(): Int // returns rows deleted } @Database(entities = [TaskEntity::class], version = 1, exportSchema = true) abstract class AppDatabase : RoomDatabase() { abstract fun taskDao(): TaskDao companion object { @Volatile private var INSTANCE: AppDatabase? = null fun getInstance(context: Context): AppDatabase { return INSTANCE ?: synchronized(this) { Room.databaseBuilder( context.applicationContext, AppDatabase::class.java, "app.db" ) .fallbackToDestructiveMigration() .build() .also { INSTANCE = it } } } } }

Room DAO functions returning Flow are automatically reactive -- they re-emit whenever the underlying table changes. No manual invalidation needed.

Recipe 11: Offline-First Write: Save to Room + Schedule WorkManager Sync

Use when the user creates/edits data and you want instant local feedback plus reliable server sync.

class TaskRepository( private val dao: TaskDao, private val workManager: WorkManager ) { fun observeTasks(): Flow<List<TaskEntity>> = dao.observeAll() suspend fun createTask(title: String, description: String) { // 1. Save locally — UI updates instantly via Flow val id = dao.upsert( TaskEntity(title = title, description = description, status = "pending") ) // 2. Schedule server sync — survives process death val syncRequest = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<SyncTaskWorker>() .setInputData(workDataOf("task_id" to id)) .setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) .build() ) .setBackoffCriteria( BackoffPolicy.EXPONENTIAL, WorkRequest.MIN_BACKOFF_MILLIS, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS ) .build() workManager.enqueue(syncRequest) } } class SyncTaskWorker( ctx: Context, params: WorkerParameters ) : CoroutineWorker(ctx, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { val taskId = inputData.getLong("task_id", -1L) if (taskId == -1L) return Result.failure() val dao = AppDatabase.getInstance(applicationContext).taskDao() val api = (applicationContext as MyApp).api val task = dao.observeById(taskId).first() ?: return Result.failure() return try { api.createTask(CreateTaskRequest(task.title, task.description)) Result.success() } catch (e: Exception) { if (runAttemptCount < 5) Result.retry() else Result.failure() } } }

The user sees instant results (Room insert triggers Flow emission). The network sync happens in the background with automatic retry. If the app is killed, WorkManager resumes the sync later.

Recipe 12: DataStore Preferences Read/Write with Flow

Use for key-value settings like theme preference, onboarding flag, or last sync time.

// Define keys as a singleton object PrefsKeys { val DARK_MODE = booleanPreferencesKey("dark_mode") val FONT_SIZE = intPreferencesKey("font_size") val USERNAME = stringPreferencesKey("username") val LAST_SYNC = longPreferencesKey("last_sync") } // Create DataStore as an extension property (top-level, once) val Context.dataStore by preferencesDataStore(name = "settings") class SettingsRepository(private val dataStore: DataStore<Preferences>) { // Read: returns Flow that emits on every change val darkMode: Flow<Boolean> = dataStore.data.map { prefs -> prefs[PrefsKeys.DARK_MODE] ?: false } val fontSize: Flow<Int> = dataStore.data.map { prefs -> prefs[PrefsKeys.FONT_SIZE] ?: 16 } // Read all settings at once val settings: Flow<UserSettings> = dataStore.data.map { prefs -> UserSettings( darkMode = prefs[PrefsKeys.DARK_MODE] ?: false, fontSize = prefs[PrefsKeys.FONT_SIZE] ?: 16, username = prefs[PrefsKeys.USERNAME] ?: "" ) } // Write: suspend function, transactional suspend fun setDarkMode(enabled: Boolean) { dataStore.edit { prefs -> prefs[PrefsKeys.DARK_MODE] = enabled } } suspend fun setFontSize(size: Int) { dataStore.edit { prefs -> prefs[PrefsKeys.FONT_SIZE] = size } } suspend fun updateLastSync() { dataStore.edit { prefs -> prefs[PrefsKeys.LAST_SYNC] = System.currentTimeMillis() } } } data class UserSettings( val darkMode: Boolean, val fontSize: Int, val username: String )

DataStore replaces SharedPreferences. It is type-safe, uses coroutines (no apply()/commit() ambiguity), and returns Flow for reactive reads. Never create more than one DataStore instance per file.

Recipe 13: Network-or-Cache Repository Pattern with TTL

Use to serve cached data instantly, then refresh from network if the cache is stale.

class UserRepository( private val api: ApiService, private val dao: UserDao, private val clock: () -> Long = System::currentTimeMillis ) { companion object { private const val CACHE_TTL_MS = 5 * 60 * 1000L // 5 minutes } fun getUser(id: String): Flow<Resource<User>> = flow { // 1. Emit cached data immediately (if available) val cached = dao.getById(id) if (cached != null) { emit(Resource.Success(cached.toDomain())) } // 2. Check if cache is fresh enough val isCacheStale = cached == null || (clock() - cached.lastFetchedAt) > CACHE_TTL_MS if (isCacheStale) { emit(Resource.Loading) try { val remote = api.getUser(id) val entity = remote.toEntity(lastFetchedAt = clock()) dao.upsert(entity) emit(Resource.Success(entity.toDomain())) } catch (e: Exception) { if (cached == null) { emit(Resource.Error(e.message ?: "Unknown error")) } // If we have stale cache, we already emitted it — user sees data } } }.flowOn(Dispatchers.IO) } sealed interface Resource<out T> { data object Loading : Resource<Nothing> data class Success<T>(val data: T) : Resource<T> data class Error(val message: String) : Resource<Nothing> }

The user sees cached data immediately, then fresh data when the network call completes. If the network fails but cache exists, the user still has data. The TTL prevents unnecessary network calls when the cache is fresh.

UI & State Recipes

Recipe 14: ViewModel with Sealed UiState + StateFlow

Use as the standard ViewModel pattern for any screen.

sealed interface ProfileUiState { data object Loading : ProfileUiState data class Success( val name: String, val email: String, val avatarUrl: String, val postCount: Int ) : ProfileUiState data class Error(val message: String) : ProfileUiState } class ProfileViewModel( private val repository: UserRepository, private val savedStateHandle: SavedStateHandle ) : ViewModel() { private val userId: String = savedStateHandle["userId"]!! private val _uiState = MutableStateFlow<ProfileUiState>(ProfileUiState.Loading) val uiState: StateFlow<ProfileUiState> = _uiState.asStateFlow() init { loadProfile() } fun loadProfile() { viewModelScope.launch { _uiState.value = ProfileUiState.Loading try { val user = repository.getUser(userId) _uiState.value = ProfileUiState.Success( name = user.name, email = user.email, avatarUrl = user.avatarUrl, postCount = user.posts.size ) } catch (e: Exception) { _uiState.value = ProfileUiState.Error( message = e.message ?: "Failed to load profile" ) } } } fun retry() = loadProfile() } // Compose UI that handles all states: @Composable fun ProfileScreen(viewModel: ProfileViewModel = hiltViewModel()) { val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() when (val state = uiState) { is ProfileUiState.Loading -> CircularProgressIndicator() is ProfileUiState.Success -> { Column { Text(state.name, style = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineMedium) Text(state.email) Text("${state.postCount} posts") } } is ProfileUiState.Error -> { Column { Text(state.message, color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.error) Button(onClick = { viewModel.retry() }) { Text("Retry") } } } } }

Sealed interfaces make when exhaustive -- the compiler ensures you handle every state. collectAsStateWithLifecycle() stops collection when the UI is not visible, saving resources.

Recipe 15: Search-as-You-Type with Debounce + flatMapLatest

Use for any search field that queries an API or database as the user types.

class SearchViewModel(private val repository: SearchRepository) : ViewModel() { private val _query = MutableStateFlow("") val searchResults: StateFlow<SearchState> = _query .debounce(300) // wait 300ms after last keystroke .distinctUntilChanged() // skip if same as previous .flatMapLatest { query -> if (query.length < 2) { flowOf(SearchState.Empty) } else { flow { emit(SearchState.Loading) try { val results = repository.search(query) emit(SearchState.Results(results)) } catch (e: Exception) { emit(SearchState.Error(e.message ?: "Search failed")) } } } } .stateIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), initialValue = SearchState.Empty ) fun onQueryChanged(newQuery: String) { _query.value = newQuery } } sealed interface SearchState { data object Empty : SearchState data object Loading : SearchState data class Results(val items: List<SearchResult>) : SearchState data class Error(val message: String) : SearchState } // Compose usage: @Composable fun SearchScreen(viewModel: SearchViewModel = hiltViewModel()) { var query by remember { mutableStateOf("") } val state by viewModel.searchResults.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() Column { TextField( value = query, onValueChange = { query = it viewModel.onQueryChanged(it) }, placeholder = { Text("Search...") } ) when (val s = state) { SearchState.Empty -> { } SearchState.Loading -> CircularProgressIndicator() is SearchState.Results -> { LazyColumn { items(s.items) { item -> Text(item.title) } } } is SearchState.Error -> Text(s.message) } } }

flatMapLatest cancels the previous search when a new query arrives. Combined with debounce, this means only the final query after the user stops typing actually triggers a network call.

Recipe 16: Pull-to-Refresh with Loading State

Use for any list screen that supports swipe-to-refresh.

class FeedViewModel(private val repository: FeedRepository) : ViewModel() { private val _isRefreshing = MutableStateFlow(false) val isRefreshing: StateFlow<Boolean> = _isRefreshing.asStateFlow() private val _feed = MutableStateFlow<List<FeedItem>>(emptyList()) val feed: StateFlow<List<FeedItem>> = _feed.asStateFlow() init { refresh() } fun refresh() { viewModelScope.launch { _isRefreshing.value = true try { _feed.value = repository.getFeed() } catch (e: Exception) { // Show snackbar or error state } finally { _isRefreshing.value = false } } } } @OptIn(ExperimentalMaterial3Api::class) @Composable fun FeedScreen(viewModel: FeedViewModel = hiltViewModel()) { val feed by viewModel.feed.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() val isRefreshing by viewModel.isRefreshing.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() val pullRefreshState = rememberPullToRefreshState() PullToRefreshBox( isRefreshing = isRefreshing, onRefresh = { viewModel.refresh() }, state = pullRefreshState ) { LazyColumn(modifier = Modifier.fillMaxSize()) { items(feed, key = { it.id }) { item -> FeedItemCard(item) } } } }

The key insight: isRefreshing is controlled by the ViewModel, not the UI. The PullToRefreshBox shows the indicator while the ViewModel sets isRefreshing = true. Always use finally to ensure the refreshing state is cleared even on error.

Recipe 17: Infinite Scroll with Paging 3 + LazyColumn

Use to load more items as the user scrolls down.

// PagingSource for a simple API with page numbers class PostPagingSource( private val api: ApiService ) : PagingSource<Int, Post>() { override fun getRefreshKey(state: PagingState<Int, Post>): Int? { return state.anchorPosition?.let { anchor -> state.closestPageToPosition(anchor)?.prevKey?.plus(1) ?: state.closestPageToPosition(anchor)?.nextKey?.minus(1) } } override suspend fun load(params: LoadParams<Int>): LoadResult<Int, Post> { val page = params.key ?: 1 return try { val posts = api.getPosts(page = page, limit = params.loadSize) LoadResult.Page( data = posts, prevKey = if (page == 1) null else page - 1, nextKey = if (posts.isEmpty()) null else page + 1 ) } catch (e: Exception) { LoadResult.Error(e) } } } // ViewModel class PostListViewModel(private val api: ApiService) : ViewModel() { val posts: Flow<PagingData<Post>> = Pager( config = PagingConfig( pageSize = 20, prefetchDistance = 5, // start loading 5 items before end initialLoadSize = 40 // first load = 2 pages ), pagingSourceFactory = { PostPagingSource(api) } ).flow.cachedIn(viewModelScope) // survive config changes } // Compose UI @Composable fun PostListScreen(viewModel: PostListViewModel = hiltViewModel()) { val lazyPagingItems = viewModel.posts.collectAsLazyPagingItems() LazyColumn { items( count = lazyPagingItems.itemCount, key = lazyPagingItems.itemKey { it.id } ) { index -> val post = lazyPagingItems[index] if (post != null) { PostCard(post) } } // Loading indicator at bottom when (lazyPagingItems.loadState.append) { is LoadState.Loading -> item { CircularProgressIndicator( modifier = Modifier.fillMaxWidth().padding(16.dp) ) } is LoadState.Error -> item { Button(onClick = { lazyPagingItems.retry() }) { Text("Retry") } } else -> { } } } }

cachedIn(viewModelScope) is critical -- it caches loaded pages so they survive configuration changes. Without it, rotating the device reloads everything from page 1.

Lifecycle Recipes

Recipe 18: repeatOnLifecycle Flow Collection

Use in Activities/Fragments to collect flows safely, stopping when the UI is not visible.

// In an Activity: class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() { private val viewModel: MainViewModel by viewModels() override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) lifecycleScope.launch { repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) { // This block runs when STARTED, cancels when STOPPED, // restarts when STARTED again. // Collect multiple flows in parallel: launch { viewModel.uiState.collect { state -> updateUi(state) } } launch { viewModel.events.collect { event -> handleEvent(event) } } } } } } // In a Fragment: class ProfileFragment : Fragment() { private val viewModel: ProfileViewModel by viewModels() override fun onViewCreated(view: View, savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onViewCreated(view, savedInstanceState) viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope.launch { viewLifecycleOwner.repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) { viewModel.uiState.collect { state -> binding.nameText.text = state.name } } } } } // Extension function to reduce boilerplate: fun <T> Flow<T>.collectIn( lifecycleOwner: LifecycleOwner, state: Lifecycle.State = Lifecycle.State.STARTED, collector: suspend (T) -> Unit ) { lifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope.launch { lifecycleOwner.repeatOnLifecycle(state) { collect(collector) } } } // Usage: viewModel.uiState.collectIn(viewLifecycleOwner) { state -> updateUi(state) }

In Fragments, always use viewLifecycleOwner (not this) because the Fragment outlives its view. Using this can lead to collecting into a destroyed view.

Recipe 19: callbackFlow to Bridge a Sensor/Location Callback

Use to convert any Android callback API into a Flow.

fun accelerometerFlow(context: Context): Flow<FloatArray> = callbackFlow { val sensorManager = context.getSystemService(Context.SENSOR_SERVICE) as SensorManager val accelerometer = sensorManager.getDefaultSensor(Sensor.TYPE_ACCELEROMETER) ?: run { close(IllegalStateException("No accelerometer")) return@callbackFlow } val listener = object : SensorEventListener { override fun onSensorChanged(event: SensorEvent) { trySend(event.values.copyOf()) // copy! values array is reused } override fun onAccuracyChanged(sensor: Sensor?, accuracy: Int) { } } sensorManager.registerListener( listener, accelerometer, SensorManager.SENSOR_DELAY_UI ) awaitClose { sensorManager.unregisterListener(listener) // cleanup on cancel } } // Usage with lifecycle-aware collection: lifecycleScope.launch { repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.RESUMED) { accelerometerFlow(requireContext()) .sample(100) // at most one event per 100ms .collect { (x, y, z) -> binding.accelText.text = "X=%.2f Y=%.2f Z=%.2f".format(x, y, z) } } } // Network connectivity Flow — another common use: fun connectivityFlow(context: Context): Flow<Boolean> = callbackFlow { val cm = context.getSystemService(Context.CONNECTIVITY_SERVICE) as ConnectivityManager val callback = object : ConnectivityManager.NetworkCallback() { override fun onAvailable(network: Network) { trySend(true) } override fun onLost(network: Network) { trySend(false) } } val request = NetworkRequest.Builder() .addCapability(NetworkCapabilities.NET_CAPABILITY_INTERNET) .build() cm.registerNetworkCallback(request, callback) // Emit current state immediately val current = cm.activeNetwork?.let { cm.getNetworkCapabilities(it)?.hasCapability(NetworkCapabilities.NET_CAPABILITY_INTERNET) } ?: false trySend(current) awaitClose { cm.unregisterNetworkCallback(callback) } }

Key rules for callbackFlow: use trySend() (non-blocking), not send(). Always provide awaitClose to unregister the callback. Copy mutable data before sending (like sensor values).

Recipe 20: suspendCancellableCoroutine to Wrap a One-Shot Callback

Use to convert a single-result callback API into a suspend function.

// Wrapping Google Play's in-app review API suspend fun requestReview(activity: Activity): Boolean { return suspendCancellableCoroutine { continuation -> val manager = ReviewManagerFactory.create(activity) val request = manager.requestReviewFlow() request.addOnSuccessListener { reviewInfo -> val flow = manager.launchReviewFlow(activity, reviewInfo) flow.addOnCompleteListener { continuation.resume(true) } } request.addOnFailureListener { e -> continuation.resume(false) // or resumeWithException(e) } continuation.invokeOnCancellation { // Clean up if the coroutine is cancelled Log.d("Review", "Review flow cancelled") } } } // Wrapping a permission request suspend fun FragmentActivity.requestPermissionSuspend( permission: String ): Boolean = suspendCancellableCoroutine { cont -> val launcher = registerForActivityResult( ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission() ) { isGranted -> cont.resume(isGranted) } launcher.launch(permission) } // Wrapping a simple animation suspend fun View.awaitFadeIn(durationMs: Long = 300) { suspendCancellableCoroutine<Unit> { cont -> alpha = 0f visibility = View.VISIBLE animate() .alpha(1f) .setDuration(durationMs) .withEndAction { cont.resume(Unit) } .start() cont.invokeOnCancellation { animate().cancel() } } } // Usage: sequential animation chain that reads like sync code viewModelScope.launch { headerView.awaitFadeIn() contentView.awaitFadeIn() buttonView.awaitFadeIn() }

suspendCancellableCoroutine bridges the callback world to the coroutine world. Always use the cancellable variant (not suspendCoroutine) so the coroutine can be cancelled. Always implement invokeOnCancellation to clean up resources.

Compose Recipes

Recipe 21: Remember + State Hoisting Pattern

Use to separate state ownership from state rendering.

// STATEFUL composable — owns its own state (use at top level) @Composable fun CounterScreen() { var count by remember { mutableIntStateOf(0) } CounterContent( count = count, onIncrement = { count++ }, onDecrement = { count-- }, onReset = { count = 0 } ) } // STATELESS composable — state is "hoisted" to caller // Reusable, testable, previewable @Composable fun CounterContent( count: Int, onIncrement: () -> Unit, onDecrement: () -> Unit, onReset: () -> Unit, modifier: Modifier = Modifier ) { Column( modifier = modifier.padding(16.dp), horizontalAlignment = Alignment.CenterHorizontally ) { Text( text = "Count: $count", style = MaterialTheme.typography.displayLarge ) Spacer(modifier = Modifier.height(16.dp)) Row(horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp)) { Button(onClick = onDecrement) { Text("-") } OutlinedButton(onClick = onReset) { Text("Reset") } Button(onClick = onIncrement) { Text("+") } } } } // Remember with a key — recalculates when key changes: @Composable fun UserAvatar(userId: String) { val color = remember(userId) { // recalculated when userId changes Color(userId.hashCode()) } Box( modifier = Modifier .size(48.dp) .clip(CircleShape) .background(color) ) }

State hoisting rule: push state UP to the lowest common ancestor that needs it. The stateless composable is a pure function of its parameters -- easy to test, preview, and reuse in different contexts.

Recipe 22: LaunchedEffect for One-Time Load vs Key-Based Reload

Use for side effects that should run when a composable enters composition or when a key changes.

// One-time load: runs once when composable enters composition @Composable fun AnalyticsScreen(screenName: String) { LaunchedEffect(Unit) { // Unit = never re-triggers Analytics.trackScreenView(screenName) } Text("Welcome to $screenName") } // Key-based reload: re-runs when userId changes @Composable fun UserProfileScreen(userId: String, viewModel: ProfileViewModel = hiltViewModel()) { LaunchedEffect(userId) { // cancels & restarts when userId changes viewModel.loadUser(userId) } val state by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() // ... render state } // Snackbar from a one-time event channel: @Composable fun OrderScreen(viewModel: OrderViewModel = hiltViewModel()) { val snackbarHostState = remember { SnackbarHostState() } LaunchedEffect(Unit) { viewModel.events.collect { event -> when (event) { is OrderEvent.OrderPlaced -> snackbarHostState.showSnackbar("Order #${event.id} placed!") is OrderEvent.Error -> snackbarHostState.showSnackbar(event.message) } } } Scaffold(snackbarHost = { SnackbarHost(snackbarHostState) }) { padding -> // ... content } } // DisposableEffect — for cleanup (register/unregister) @Composable fun LifecycleLogger(lifecycleOwner: LifecycleOwner = LocalLifecycleOwner.current) { DisposableEffect(lifecycleOwner) { val observer = LifecycleEventObserver { _, event -> Log.d("Lifecycle", event.name) } lifecycleOwner.lifecycle.addObserver(observer) onDispose { lifecycleOwner.lifecycle.removeObserver(observer) } } }

LaunchedEffect(key): when key changes, the previous coroutine is cancelled and a new one starts. Use Unit as the key for "run once." Use DisposableEffect when you need onDispose cleanup (like removing listeners).

Recipe 23: Custom Modifier (Shimmer Loading Effect)

Use to create reusable visual modifiers.

fun Modifier.shimmer( isLoading: Boolean, shimmerColor: Color = Color.LightGray, durationMs: Int = 1200 ): Modifier = composed { // composed {} allows remember/state inside a modifier if (!isLoading) return@composed this val transition = rememberInfiniteTransition(label = "shimmer") val translateAnim by transition.animateFloat( initialValue = -1f, targetValue = 2f, animationSpec = infiniteRepeatable( animation = tween(durationMs, easing = LinearEasing), repeatMode = RepeatMode.Restart ), label = "shimmerTranslate" ) this .clip(RoundedCornerShape(4.dp)) .drawWithContent { drawContent() val brush = Brush.linearGradient( colors = listOf( shimmerColor.copy(alpha = 0.3f), shimmerColor.copy(alpha = 0.7f), shimmerColor.copy(alpha = 0.3f) ), start = Offset(size.width * translateAnim, 0f), end = Offset(size.width * (translateAnim + 1f), size.height) ) drawRect(brush = brush) } } // Usage: @Composable fun ArticleCardPlaceholder(isLoading: Boolean) { Column(modifier = Modifier.padding(16.dp)) { Box( modifier = Modifier .fillMaxWidth() .height(200.dp) .shimmer(isLoading) .background(Color.LightGray, RoundedCornerShape(8.dp)) ) Spacer(modifier = Modifier.height(8.dp)) Box( modifier = Modifier .fillMaxWidth(0.7f) .height(20.dp) .shimmer(isLoading) .background(Color.LightGray, RoundedCornerShape(4.dp)) ) Spacer(modifier = Modifier.height(4.dp)) Box( modifier = Modifier .fillMaxWidth(0.5f) .height(16.dp) .shimmer(isLoading) .background(Color.LightGray, RoundedCornerShape(4.dp)) ) } }

Modifier.composed { } lets you use remember and composable state inside a modifier factory. The shimmer effect uses an infinite animation that translates a gradient across the surface.

Recipe 24: Bottom Navigation with NavigationBar + NavHost + Multi-Back-Stack

Use for apps with 3-5 bottom tabs, each with its own back stack.

// Define routes enum class TopLevelRoute( val label: String, val icon: ImageVector, val route: String ) { Home("Home", Icons.Default.Home, "home"), Search("Search", Icons.Default.Search, "search"), Profile("Profile", Icons.Default.Person, "profile") } @Composable fun MainApp() { val navController = rememberNavController() val currentBackStackEntry by navController.currentBackStackEntryAsState() val currentRoute = currentBackStackEntry?.destination?.route Scaffold( bottomBar = { NavigationBar { TopLevelRoute.entries.forEach { destination -> NavigationBarItem( selected = currentRoute == destination.route, onClick = { navController.navigate(destination.route) { // Pop up to start to avoid stacking tabs popUpTo(navController.graph.findStartDestination().id) { saveState = true // save back stack state } launchSingleTop = true // no duplicate tabs restoreState = true // restore saved state } }, icon = { Icon(destination.icon, contentDescription = destination.label) }, label = { Text(destination.label) } ) } } } ) { innerPadding -> NavHost( navController = navController, startDestination = TopLevelRoute.Home.route, modifier = Modifier.padding(innerPadding) ) { composable(TopLevelRoute.Home.route) { HomeScreen(onItemClick = { id -> navController.navigate("home/detail/$id") }) } composable("home/detail/{id}") { backStackEntry -> DetailScreen(id = backStackEntry.arguments?.getString("id") ?: "") } composable(TopLevelRoute.Search.route) { SearchScreen() } composable(TopLevelRoute.Profile.route) { ProfileScreen() } } } }

The three flags -- saveState, restoreState, and popUpTo(startDestination) -- enable multi-back-stack behavior. Each tab remembers its navigation history independently.

Architecture Recipes

Recipe 25: Hilt Module Providing Retrofit + Room + Repository

Use as the standard DI setup for a production app.

@Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object NetworkModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideOkHttpClient(): OkHttpClient { return OkHttpClient.Builder() .addInterceptor(HttpLoggingInterceptor().apply { level = if (BuildConfig.DEBUG) HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.BODY else HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.NONE }) .connectTimeout(15, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .readTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .build() } @Provides @Singleton fun provideRetrofit(okHttpClient: OkHttpClient): Retrofit { return Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/v1/") .client(okHttpClient) .addConverterFactory(Json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType())) .build() } @Provides @Singleton fun provideApiService(retrofit: Retrofit): ApiService { return retrofit.create(ApiService::class.java) } } @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object DatabaseModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideDatabase(@ApplicationContext context: Context): AppDatabase { return Room.databaseBuilder( context, AppDatabase::class.java, "app.db" ) .fallbackToDestructiveMigration() .build() } @Provides fun provideTaskDao(db: AppDatabase): TaskDao = db.taskDao() @Provides fun provideUserDao(db: AppDatabase): UserDao = db.userDao() } @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object RepositoryModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideTaskRepository( taskDao: TaskDao, apiService: ApiService ): TaskRepository { return TaskRepository(taskDao, apiService) } } // Application class @HiltAndroidApp class MyApp : Application() // Activity @AndroidEntryPoint class MainActivity : ComponentActivity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) setContent { MyApp() } } } // ViewModel — just add @HiltViewModel and @Inject constructor @HiltViewModel class TaskViewModel @Inject constructor( private val repository: TaskRepository ) : ViewModel() { // Hilt automatically provides the repository }

Hilt's @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) means these objects live for the entire app lifecycle. DAOs are not scoped as @Singleton because they are lightweight -- the AppDatabase instance (which holds the connection) is the expensive part.

Recipe 26: Manual DI Without Hilt (For Understanding)

Use to understand what Hilt does under the hood, or for small projects where Hilt is overkill.

// AppContainer: manual singleton wiring class AppContainer(private val context: Context) { // Lazy = created on first access, then reused val okHttpClient: OkHttpClient by lazy { OkHttpClient.Builder() .addInterceptor(HttpLoggingInterceptor()) .build() } val retrofit: Retrofit by lazy { Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/v1/") .client(okHttpClient) .addConverterFactory(Json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType())) .build() } val api: ApiService by lazy { retrofit.create(ApiService::class.java) } val database: AppDatabase by lazy { Room.databaseBuilder(context, AppDatabase::class.java, "app.db") .fallbackToDestructiveMigration() .build() } val taskRepository: TaskRepository by lazy { TaskRepository(database.taskDao(), api) } val userRepository: UserRepository by lazy { UserRepository(database.userDao(), api) } } // Application class MyApp : Application() { lateinit var container: AppContainer private set override fun onCreate() { super.onCreate() container = AppContainer(this) } } // ViewModel with factory (no Hilt) class TaskViewModel( private val repository: TaskRepository ) : ViewModel() { class Factory(private val repository: TaskRepository) : ViewModelProvider.Factory { override fun <T : ViewModel> create(modelClass: Class<T>): T { @Suppress("UNCHECKED_CAST") return TaskViewModel(repository) as T } } } // Usage in Activity class TaskActivity : AppCompatActivity() { private val viewModel: TaskViewModel by viewModels { val app = application as MyApp TaskViewModel.Factory(app.container.taskRepository) } }

This is what Hilt generates for you. Understanding manual DI makes Hilt's magic transparent: @Singleton = by lazy, @Provides = a factory function, @Inject constructor = constructor parameters wired from the container.

Recipe 27: CoroutineWorker with Retry and Progress Reporting

Use for long-running background tasks that need to report progress to the UI.

class UploadWorker( ctx: Context, params: WorkerParameters ) : CoroutineWorker(ctx, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { val filePaths = inputData.getStringArray("file_paths") ?: return Result.failure() // Show as foreground service (required for long tasks on Android 12+) setForeground(createForegroundInfo("Uploading...")) var uploaded = 0 for (path in filePaths) { try { uploadFile(path) uploaded++ // Report progress setProgress(workDataOf( "uploaded" to uploaded, "total" to filePaths.size )) } catch (e: Exception) { if (runAttemptCount < 3) { return Result.retry() // WorkManager handles backoff } return Result.failure(workDataOf("error" to e.message)) } } return Result.success(workDataOf("uploaded" to uploaded)) } private fun createForegroundInfo(title: String): ForegroundInfo { val channelId = "upload_channel" val notification = NotificationCompat.Builder(applicationContext, channelId) .setContentTitle(title) .setSmallIcon(R.drawable.ic_upload) .setOngoing(true) .build() return ForegroundInfo( 1001, notification, ServiceInfo.FOREGROUND_SERVICE_TYPE_DATA_SYNC ) } private suspend fun uploadFile(path: String) { withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { // actual upload logic } } } // Enqueue and observe: fun startUpload(context: Context, files: List<String>) { val request = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<UploadWorker>() .setInputData(workDataOf("file_paths" to files.toTypedArray())) .setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) .build() ) .setBackoffCriteria(BackoffPolicy.EXPONENTIAL, 30, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .build() WorkManager.getInstance(context) .enqueueUniqueWork("upload", ExistingWorkPolicy.KEEP, request) } // Observe progress in ViewModel: class UploadViewModel(application: Application) : AndroidViewModel(application) { val uploadProgress = WorkManager.getInstance(application) .getWorkInfosForUniqueWorkLiveData("upload") .map { workInfos -> workInfos.firstOrNull()?.let { info -> val uploaded = info.progress.getInt("uploaded", 0) val total = info.progress.getInt("total", 0) UploadProgress(uploaded, total, info.state) } } } data class UploadProgress(val uploaded: Int, val total: Int, val state: WorkInfo.State)

setForeground() promotes the worker to a foreground service, which is required for long-running tasks on Android 12+. setProgress() lets the UI observe partial progress in real-time.

Testing Recipes

Recipe 28: ViewModel Test with Turbine + runTest

Use for testing ViewModel state emissions over time.

class TaskViewModelTest { @get:Rule val mainDispatcherRule = MainDispatcherRule() // replaces Main with TestDispatcher private lateinit var fakeRepo: FakeTaskRepository private lateinit var viewModel: TaskViewModel @Before fun setup() { fakeRepo = FakeTaskRepository() viewModel = TaskViewModel(fakeRepo) } @Test fun `loadTasks emits Loading then Success`() = runTest { fakeRepo.setTasks(listOf( Task(id = "1", title = "Buy groceries"), Task(id = "2", title = "Walk dog") )) viewModel.uiState.test { // Turbine extension assertEquals(TaskUiState.Loading, awaitItem()) viewModel.loadTasks() val success = awaitItem() as TaskUiState.Success assertEquals(2, success.tasks.size) assertEquals("Buy groceries", success.tasks[0].title) cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } } @Test fun `loadTasks emits Error on failure`() = runTest { fakeRepo.setShouldFail(true) viewModel.uiState.test { assertEquals(TaskUiState.Loading, awaitItem()) viewModel.loadTasks() val error = awaitItem() as TaskUiState.Error assertTrue(error.message.contains("Failed")) cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } } } // MainDispatcherRule — required for testing viewModelScope class MainDispatcherRule( private val testDispatcher: TestDispatcher = UnconfinedTestDispatcher() ) : TestWatcher() { override fun starting(description: Description) { Dispatchers.setMain(testDispatcher) } override fun finished(description: Description) { Dispatchers.resetMain() } }

Turbine's .test { } collects emissions and lets you assert them in order with awaitItem(). MainDispatcherRule replaces Dispatchers.Main with a test dispatcher so viewModelScope.launch runs synchronously.

Recipe 29: Fake Repository for Unit Tests

Use to decouple tests from real network/database implementations.

// Interface that both real and fake implement interface TaskRepository { fun observeTasks(): Flow<List<Task>> suspend fun getTask(id: String): Task? suspend fun createTask(title: String): Task suspend fun deleteTask(id: String) } // Fake implementation for tests class FakeTaskRepository : TaskRepository { private val tasks = MutableStateFlow<List<Task>>(emptyList()) private var shouldFail = false private var failureException: Exception = RuntimeException("Failed") // Test helpers fun setTasks(newTasks: List<Task>) { tasks.value = newTasks } fun setShouldFail(fail: Boolean, ex: Exception = RuntimeException("Failed")) { shouldFail = fail failureException = ex } override fun observeTasks(): Flow<List<Task>> = tasks override suspend fun getTask(id: String): Task? { if (shouldFail) throw failureException return tasks.value.find { it.id == id } } override suspend fun createTask(title: String): Task { if (shouldFail) throw failureException val task = Task(id = UUID.randomUUID().toString(), title = title) tasks.value = tasks.value + task return task } override suspend fun deleteTask(id: String) { if (shouldFail) throw failureException tasks.value = tasks.value.filter { it.id != id } } } // Usage in tests: @Test fun `createTask adds task to list`() = runTest { val repo = FakeTaskRepository() repo.observeTasks().test { assertEquals(emptyList<Task>(), awaitItem()) repo.createTask("New task") val updated = awaitItem() assertEquals(1, updated.size) assertEquals("New task", updated[0].title) cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } } @Test fun `createTask throws when shouldFail is true`() = runTest { val repo = FakeTaskRepository() repo.setShouldFail(true) assertThrows<RuntimeException> { repo.createTask("Will fail") } }

Fakes are preferred over mocks because they implement real logic (backed by in-memory data). This makes tests more meaningful: you're testing behavior, not interaction counts. The shouldFail flag lets you test error paths trivially.

Recipe 30: ComposeTestRule UI Test

Use for testing Compose UI interactions and state rendering.

class TaskListScreenTest { @get:Rule val composeTestRule = createComposeRule() @Test fun `shows loading indicator initially`() { composeTestRule.setContent { TaskListContent( state = TaskUiState.Loading, onTaskClick = {}, onAddTask = {}, onRefresh = {} ) } composeTestRule .onNodeWithTag("loading_indicator") .assertIsDisplayed() } @Test fun `shows tasks when loaded`() { val tasks = listOf( Task("1", "Buy groceries"), Task("2", "Walk dog"), Task("3", "Write tests") ) composeTestRule.setContent { TaskListContent( state = TaskUiState.Success(tasks), onTaskClick = {}, onAddTask = {}, onRefresh = {} ) } composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Buy groceries").assertIsDisplayed() composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Walk dog").assertIsDisplayed() composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Write tests").assertIsDisplayed() } @Test fun `clicking task triggers callback`() { var clickedId: String? = null val tasks = listOf(Task("1", "Buy groceries")) composeTestRule.setContent { TaskListContent( state = TaskUiState.Success(tasks), onTaskClick = { clickedId = it }, onAddTask = {}, onRefresh = {} ) } composeTestRule .onNodeWithText("Buy groceries") .performClick() assertEquals("1", clickedId) } @Test fun `shows error with retry button`() { var retried = false composeTestRule.setContent { TaskListContent( state = TaskUiState.Error("Network error"), onTaskClick = {}, onAddTask = {}, onRefresh = { retried = true } ) } composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Network error").assertIsDisplayed() composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Retry").performClick() assertTrue(retried) } @Test fun `add button exists and is clickable`() { var addClicked = false composeTestRule.setContent { TaskListContent( state = TaskUiState.Success(emptyList()), onTaskClick = {}, onAddTask = { addClicked = true }, onRefresh = {} ) } composeTestRule .onNodeWithContentDescription("Add task") .performClick() assertTrue(addClicked) } }

Test the stateless composable (TaskListContent), not the screen with ViewModel. This isolates UI logic from data fetching. Use testTag for elements without visible text (loading spinners), and contentDescription for icons and images.

questions.title: What Does This callbackFlow Do?
questions.description: What does this code produce and when does cleanup happen?
options: It crashes because you can't use Handler inside callbackFlow
It emits incrementing Long values (0, 1, 2, ...) at the given interval, and removes the Handler callbacks when the Flow collector is cancelled
It emits once and completes
It emits but never cleans up the Handler
questions.explanation: The callbackFlow bridges the Handler callback into a Flow. Each time the Runnable fires, it sends the next count via trySend() and reschedules itself. When the Flow collector cancels (e.g., lifecycle stop), awaitClose runs and removes all pending callbacks. This is the standard pattern for converting any repeating callback into a Flow.
hints: Look at what trySend does and what awaitClose provides

questions.title: What's the Bug in This Retry?
questions.description: This retry function has a subtle bug. What is it?
options: It works correctly — no bug
It catches CancellationException, which prevents coroutine cancellation from working properly
It only retries twice instead of three times
The !! operator will cause a NullPointerException
questions.explanation: Catching Exception also catches CancellationException, which is how Kotlin coroutines signal cancellation. If the coroutine is cancelled (e.g., ViewModel cleared), the function will retry instead of stopping. Fix: add if (e is CancellationException) throw e inside the catch, or catch only specific exception types like IOException.
hints: What exception type does coroutine cancellation use?
Does the catch block discriminate between error types?

questions.title: flatMapLatest Behavior
questions.description: The user types "ab" then "abc" quickly. How many API calls are made?
options: 2 calls: one for "ab" and one for "abc"
1 call: only "abc" — debounce waits 300ms and flatMapLatest cancels the "ab" call if "abc" arrives before it completes
3 calls: one per character
0 calls: the debounce swallows everything
questions.explanation: The user types "ab", then quickly types "abc". The debounce waits 300ms after the last keystroke. Since "abc" arrives within 300ms of "ab", only "abc" passes through. Even if "ab" somehow made it through, flatMapLatest would cancel its flow when "abc" arrives. The result: exactly one API call for "abc".
hints: debounce waits for the user to stop typing
flatMapLatest cancels the previous flow when a new value arrives

questions.title: What's Wrong With This remember?
questions.description: This composable has a bug. What happens when userId changes?
options: It works perfectly — loads and displays the user
It shows the wrong user: remember without a key keeps the old state, and LaunchedEffect(Unit) never re-fetches when userId changes
It crashes with a NullPointerException
It recomposes infinitely
questions.explanation: Two bugs: (1) remember { } without a key means the state survives even when userId changes, showing stale data. Fix: remember(userId) { ... }. (2) LaunchedEffect(Unit) runs only once, not when userId changes. Fix: LaunchedEffect(userId). Together, navigating to a different user shows the previous user's data forever.
hints: What is the key for remember? What is the key for LaunchedEffect?

questions.title: WorkManager Constraint Behavior
questions.description: The user enqueues this worker while on mobile data (not Wi-Fi) and the device is not charging. What happens?
options: The work runs immediately — constraints are just hints
The work is queued but does NOT run until the device is on Wi-Fi AND charging. WorkManager persists the request across app restarts and process death
The work fails with a ConstraintNotMetException
The work runs on mobile data since it's the only network available
questions.explanation: WorkManager constraints are hard requirements, not hints. The work is enqueued and persisted to an internal SQLite database. It will not execute until ALL constraints are simultaneously met (Wi-Fi connected AND charger plugged in). This survives app kills and device reboots. The work could run hours or days later when conditions are finally met.
hints: WorkManager is persistent — what backs its queue?

questions.title: Interceptor Chain Order
questions.description: In which order do these interceptors run, and what does each see?
questions.code:
val client = OkHttpClient.Builder()
    .addInterceptor(AuthInterceptor())       // adds Authorization header
    .addInterceptor(LoggingInterceptor())     // logs request/response
    .addNetworkInterceptor(CacheInterceptor()) // adds Cache-Control
    .build()
options: Auth → Logging → Cache. Logging sees the Authorization header. Cache sees the final request after redirects.
Cache → Logging → Auth
All three run in parallel
Auth → Cache → Logging. Logging sees the cached response.
questions.explanation: Application interceptors (addInterceptor) run in order first: Auth adds the header, then Logging sees the request WITH the Authorization header. Network interceptors (addNetworkInterceptor) run after OkHttp's internal redirect/retry logic, so Cache sees the final wire-level request. This distinction matters: logging as an application interceptor shows your original request, while logging as a network interceptor shows what actually goes over the wire (after redirects).
hints: Application interceptors run before network interceptors
What does each interceptor see in the chain?

questions.title: Room Flow Auto-Update Bug
questions.description: The developer expects the UI to update when a task status changes. Does it?
options: Yes, Room automatically notifies the MutableStateFlow
No — the DAO returns a one-shot List, not a Flow. After markDone() updates the database, _tasks still holds the old list. Fix: change the DAO to return Flow<List<TaskEntity>> and collect it
Yes, but only after a 5-second delay
It crashes because you can't call a suspend DAO function from viewModelScope
questions.explanation: The DAO function getAllTasks() returns List<TaskEntity> — a one-shot query. It reads the current data and is done. Room's reactivity only works when the DAO returns Flow<List<TaskEntity>>. With Flow, Room internally uses InvalidationTracker to re-query and emit new data whenever the table changes. The fix: change the DAO to fun observeAll(): Flow<List<TaskEntity>> and collect it in the ViewModel.
hints: What return type makes Room reactive?
Compare suspend fun returning List vs fun returning Flow

questions.title: collectAsStateWithLifecycle vs collectAsState
questions.description: What is the practical difference in this Compose code?
options: No practical difference — they are aliases
Option A keeps collecting even when the app is in the background (wasting resources). Option B stops collecting when the lifecycle drops below STARTED, saving CPU/battery/network.
Option B crashes on API < 28
Option A is faster because it skips lifecycle checks
questions.explanation: collectAsState() collects the Flow as long as the composable is in the composition — even when the app is backgrounded. This means your Flow (which might trigger network calls, sensor reads, or location updates) keeps running invisibly. collectAsStateWithLifecycle() stops collection when the lifecycle drops below STARTED (i.e., app goes to background), and restarts when the app returns to the foreground. Always prefer collectAsStateWithLifecycle().
hints: What happens to Flow collection when the user presses Home?
